questions.question_text: Which activity requires a Montana real estate license?
distractors: Giving free advice to a neighbor
questions.explanation: Negotiating a real estate sale for compensation on behalf of another person requires a Montana real estate license. Selling one's own property, giving free advice, and appraising are not activities requiring a real estate license.

questions.question_text: A Montana investor hires an individual who holds no license of any kind to collect rent on 10 rental units for a fee. Is a license required?
questions.correct_answer: Yes — a property manager license (or a real estate license) is required
distractors: No, rent collection is exempt from licensing
Only if managing more than 20 rental units
No, if the owner signs a written management agreement
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-56-103, a person may not practice as a property manager unless actively licensed, and collecting rents on others' property is property management (MCA 37-56-101). A licensed real estate broker or salesperson may also act as a property manager without a separate property manager license (MCA 37-56-104(2)). Montana does exempt a person who leases no more than four residential units (MCA 37-56-104(1)(e)), but 10 units for compensation requires licensure.

questions.question_text: Which person is EXEMPT from Montana real estate licensing requirements?
questions.correct_answer: An owner selling their own property
distractors: A paid referral agent for buyers
A friend negotiating a sale for a fee
A consultant listing homes for commission
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-51-103, property owners selling their own real estate are exempt from licensing. Persons who negotiate, list, or refer for compensation on behalf of others must hold a license.

questions.question_text: A property owner hires someone who holds no license of any kind to manage five residential units for a monthly fee. What does Montana law require?
questions.correct_answer: The person must become licensed — as a property manager or as a real estate broker or salesperson
distractors: Nothing — residential rentals are unlicensed activity
Nothing — the exemption for four or fewer residential units applies
questions.explanation: Property management is licensed under MCA Title 37, chapter 56: a person may not practice as a property manager unless actively licensed (37-56-103). A licensed real estate broker or salesperson may also act as a property manager (37-56-104(2)); a salesperson doing so still needs a supervising broker. The statutory exemption covers only a person who leases no more than four residential real estate units (37-56-104(1)(e)) — five units exceeds it, so the person must hold a license.

questions.question_text: A homeowner asks a friend who holds no license to list her house for sale and offers a $2,000 fee. Is this lawful?
questions.correct_answer: No, listing for compensation requires a license
distractors: Yes, friends are exempt from licensing rules
Yes, if the fee is under $5,000 total
Only if the friend advertises the property
questions.explanation: Listing real property for sale on behalf of another person for compensation is a licensed activity in Montana regardless of the relationship between the parties. The friend would need a valid real estate license.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum age to obtain a Montana salesperson license?
questions.explanation: Montana requires salesperson license applicants to be at least 18 years of age. This is consistent with many other states' minimum age requirements for real estate licensure.

questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education must a Montana salesperson candidate complete?
questions.explanation: Montana requires 70 hours of approved pre-license education for salesperson applicants. This education must be completed before the candidate can sit for the licensing examination.

questions.question_text: A salesperson has been actively licensed for 18 months and wants to become a broker. Does she meet the experience requirement?
questions.correct_answer: No, she needs 2 years of active experience
distractors: Yes, if she completes extra education
No, 3 years of experience is required
questions.explanation: Montana requires a minimum of 2 years of active experience as a licensed salesperson before an individual can apply for a broker license. At 18 months, she has not yet met this requirement.

questions.question_text: A newly licensed salesperson wants to begin practicing immediately. What must she have in addition to passing the exam?
distractors: A business license from the county
questions.explanation: A Montana salesperson must be employed by or under contract with a licensed broker who holds a supervising broker endorsement before engaging in any real estate activity (MCA 37-51-302; ARM 24.210.611). The salesperson cannot practice independently; all activity must be under the supervising broker's oversight.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum education prerequisite for a Montana salesperson applicant?
questions.explanation: Montana requires salesperson applicants to have completed at least 2 years of high school education. This is a lower threshold than many states that require a full diploma or equivalent.

questions.question_text: When must Montana real estate licenses be renewed each year?
questions.explanation: Montana real estate licenses must be renewed annually by October 31. The licensing year runs from November 1 through October 31 of the following year.

questions.question_text: A Montana salesperson fails to renew by October 31. What happens to her license?
questions.correct_answer: It lapses — she has 45 days to renew late before it expires
distractors: It remains fully active for 12 more months
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-1-141, a license not renewed by the renewal date (October 31) lapses; the licensee may reactivate it within 45 days by completing renewal requirements and paying a late fee. If not renewed within that window the license expires — practicing on an expired license is unlicensed practice — and a license not renewed within 2 years of the renewal date automatically terminates, requiring a brand-new application. 'Inactive' is a different, voluntarily elected status; failing to renew does not convert a license to inactive.

questions.question_text: A Montana salesperson completed 8 hours of CE by September. How many more hours are needed before October 31?
questions.explanation: Montana requires 12 hours of continuing education per licensing year, which ends October 31. With 8 hours completed, the salesperson needs 4 more hours to meet the 12-hour annual requirement.

questions.question_text: How many hours of continuing education does Montana require annually for license renewal?
questions.explanation: Montana requires 12 hours of approved continuing education annually for license renewal. Supervising brokers must complete at least 4 of those 12 hours in supervision-related education.

questions.question_text: Montana salesperson pre-license education must have been completed within what period before the license application is submitted?
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.611(8) requires proof of completing 70 hours of approved prelicensing education obtained within the 24 months immediately preceding submission of the license application. Exam results have their own separate clock: candidates who pass the exam must apply for licensure within 12 months of the examination date (ARM 24.210.611(3)).

questions.question_text: A Montana salesperson changes her home address. How quickly must she notify the Board?
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-1-143 (enacted by HB 276, Ch. 247, L. 2025), Montana licensees must report a change of address within 30 days after the change. A broker relocating the brokerage office itself has a different clock: notice before removal or within 10 days after (MCA 37-51-308). Failure to provide timely notice may result in disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A salesperson leaves her sponsoring broker but has not yet affiliated with a new one. What is her license status?
questions.explanation: When a Montana salesperson is without a supervising broker, her license is automatically placed on inactive status (MCA 37-51-309(3)) until a successor supervising broker files written acceptance with the department. She cannot conduct any real estate activities during this period.

questions.question_text: A Montana supervising broker decides to terminate supervision of one of her salespersons. What does the license law require the broker to do?
questions.correct_answer: Notify the salesperson in advance of, or concurrently with, notifying the Board
distractors: Return the salesperson's license certificate to the Board within 24 hours
Transfer the salesperson's license directly to a new broker
File a termination report with the local MLS
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-51-309(4), a supervising broker who terminates supervision shall notify the salesperson in advance or concurrently with notification to the board. The salesperson's license then goes on inactive status until a successor supervising broker files written acceptance. Montana licensing is administered electronically; the law no longer requires physically returning license certificates to the Board.

questions.question_text: Where must a Montana licensee's license certificate be kept?
distractors: Kept in the licensee's personal files
questions.explanation: Montana requires that license certificates be displayed at the brokerage office where the licensee is affiliated. This allows consumers and the Board to verify active licensure.

questions.question_text: A supervising broker closes her firm while two salespersons are affiliated with her. What happens to the salespersons' licenses?
questions.correct_answer: They go on inactive status until a new supervising broker accepts supervision
distractors: The broker must mail the license certificates back to the Board
questions.explanation: A Montana salesperson may practice only under a supervising broker (MCA 37-51-309). A broker ending supervision must notify each salesperson and the Board, and a salesperson without a supervising broker is automatically put on inactive status until a successor supervising broker files written acceptance with the department. No physical return of license certificates is required under current law.
